mnemonics.title: Cyclic Group Definition
mnemonics.content: CANDY: Cyclic All Nesting Derived Yes - A cyclic group can be generated by repeated operation on ONE element, and every element can be derived from this generator.

mnemonics.title: Generator Count
mnemonics.content: φ(n) Generators = Just φind out how many coprimes! For order n, count numbers less than n that share no common factors with n (except 1).

mnemonics.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
mnemonics.content: Prime Power Minus One: For prime p, a^(p-1) = 1 mod p. Think: 'P-1 person shares 1 pizza equally'.

mnemonics.title: Order Formula
mnemonics.content: Order of g^k = n / gcd(k,n) - Divide the group order by the greatest common divisor of k and n to find the element's order.

acronyms.usage: Remembering cyclic groups involve repeated application of indices/exponents to generate all elements.

acronyms.expansion: Generates Ordered Residues Traversing Entire Range
acronyms.usage: A generator 'g' produces all elements as powers, traversing the entire range of residues in the group.

acronyms.expansion: φunction that counts numbers relatively prime to n
acronyms.usage: Used to count generators: φ(n) tells us how many generators a cyclic group of order n has.

acronyms.expansion: Largest Greatest Group Partition Divides Evenly
acronyms.usage: The order of any subgroup divides the order of the group - crucial for cyclic group subgroup analysis.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a clock with n hours. Starting at 12 (identity), each step moves you to the next hour. After n steps you return to 12. This represents Z_n perfectly.
Imagine a wheel with the generator as the hub. All elements radiate outward from this single point through repeated operations.
Like Russian nesting dolls, subgroups fit inside each other. The number and size of these 'dolls' are determined by the divisors of n.
Think of gears with different teeth. An element of order d completes a cycle after d steps, like a d-toothed gear returning to its starting position.

analogies.analogy: Like a clock - starting at 12 and adding hours repeatedly cycles through all 12 positions before returning to 12.
Like the sun in a solar system - all planets (elements) are reached by repeatedly moving away from (powers of) the sun.
Like the length of a rope - the order tells you how many times you need to wrap it around a pole before it ends up at the same position.
Like concentric circles - starting from the center (generator) and going around creates smaller circles (subgroups) of different radii (orders).
